title: Map: Celsius to Fahrenheit
description: Given an array of temperatures in Celsius, return a new array where each temperature is converted to Fahrenheit. Formula: `(C * 9/5) + 32`.
whatShouldHappen: Use .map() to iterate over the array.
Apply the conversion formula to each element.
Return the new array of Fahrenheit values.
patternExplanation: .map() is the standard tool for transforming Every item in an array into something else while keeping the array length the same.

title: Filter: Active Users
description: Given an array of user objects, return a new array containing only the users who are active (where `isActive` is true).
whatShouldHappen: Use .filter() on the users array.
The callback should return the value of the `isActive` property.
Return the filtered array.
patternExplanation: .filter() creates a subset of the original array based on a boolean condition.

title: Find: User by ID
description: Given an array of users and an ID, return the first user object that matches the ID. If no user is found, return `undefined`.
whatShouldHappen: Use .find() to locate the user.
Compare each user's `id` property to the target `id`.
Return the found object.
patternExplanation: .find() is more efficient than .filter() when you only need a single item, as it stops iterating as soon as a match is found.

title: Every: Are All Even?
description: Check if every number in an array is even. Return `true` if they are, and `false` if at least one is odd.
whatShouldHappen: Use .every() on the numbers array.
Check if each number % 2 is equal to 0.
Return the result.
patternExplanation: .every() returns true if the callback returns true for ALL elements. If it finds one false, it stops and returns false immediately.

title: Some: Has Admin?
description: Given an array of users, return `true` if at least one user has the role `'admin'`. Otherwise, return `false`.
whatShouldHappen: Use .some() on the users array.
Check if user.role is equal to 'admin'.
Return the result.
patternExplanation: .some() returns true if at least ONE element satisfies the condition. It stops iterating as soon as it finds a match.

title: FindIndex: First Vowel
description: Given an array of characters, return the index of the first vowel ('a', 'e', 'i', 'o', 'u'). If no vowel exists, return -1.
whatShouldHappen: Use .findIndex() on the array.
Check if the character (lowercase) is included in the string 'aeiou'.
Return the resulting index.
patternExplanation: .findIndex() is similar to .find(), but it returns the numeric index of the first match instead of the value itself.

title: ForEach: Sum Positives
description: Given an array of numbers, use `.forEach()` to calculate the sum of all positive numbers (> 0). Return the sum.
whatShouldHappen: Initialize a `sum` variable to 0.
Call .forEach() on the array.
In the callback, check if the current number is positive.
If positive, add it to the `sum` variable.
Return the `sum` after the loop.
patternExplanation: .forEach() is used for side effects. While .reduce() is usually better for summing, forEach is a clear way to perform logic on every item without creating a new array.

title: Chained: Top Performers
description: Given an array of student objects `{ name: string, score: number }`, return an array of names for students who scored 80 or higher.
whatShouldHappen: Use .filter() to keep only students with score >= 80.
Chain .map() to extract only the 'name' property from those filtered objects.
Return the resulting array of strings.
patternExplanation: One of the biggest strengths of functional array methods is that they can be chained together to perform complex data pipelines in a readable way.

title: Map: Numbered List
description: Given an array of strings, return a new array where each string is prefixed with its 1-based index (e.g., ['apple'] becomes ['1. apple']).
whatShouldHappen: Use the second argument of the .map() callback, which is the current `index`.
Return a template string combining `index + 1` and the current item.
Return the new array.
patternExplanation: All iteration methods (map, filter, forEach, etc.) provide the index as a second argument to the callback.

title: Filter: Unique Values
description: Given an array of values, return a new array containing only the unique elements. Use `.filter()` and `.indexOf()` to solve this without using a Set.
whatShouldHappen: Use .filter() on the array.
The callback receives the `item` and its current `index`.
Check if `arr.indexOf(item)` is equal to the current `index`.
Since .indexOf() always returns the *first* occurrence, this condition will only be true for the first time an element appears.
Return the filtered array.
patternExplanation: Comparing the current index to the indexOf() the element is a classic clever way to filter duplicates in a single chain without extra data structures.
